o in response? As a senator-elect, I=E2=80=99m looking at hiring a Senate s=
taff, and every senator typically has legal counsel on staff. And you bette=
r believe that=E2=80=99s an important position to me right now.

You lived through Trump I=E2=80=94that=E2=80=99s how you came to office, in=
 a lot of ways. What=E2=80=99s your mindset going into Trump II?

We know that Trump is extremely good at throwing out red meat on five or 10=
 different issues in a given day. One of the mistakes Democrats made back i=
n his first term was not separating the wheat from chaff. I thought a lot a=
bout that from a national security [perspective]. I gamed out a kind of dec=
ision tree on which issues I really must engage in, and which issues are mo=
re fleeting.

When Trump does something on a given day that I don=E2=80=99t agree with, I=
 think, is it tactical? Is it strategic? And then: Is it reversible or irre=
versible? And something that=E2=80=99s irreversible and strategic should ge=
t my attention, and something that=E2=80=99s tactical and reversible may no=
t rise to the occasion. If we try to push back on everything, we=E2=80=99ll=
 be effective at nothing.

And obviously, as a national security person, what
happens in the use of the active duty military is very important, and both =
strategic and irreversible. If we start deeply politicizing the military, u=
sing it as an arm of retribution, or contravening the Constitution by activ=
ating [the military] inside the United States, these are things that litera=
lly contravene the Constitution. I=E2=80=99m trying to figure out how to be=
 most effective against those threats.

You=E2=80=99ll be in the minority as an incoming senator. What role can a s=
enator play in pushing back against Trump?

I can=E2=80=99t speak from experience, since I=E2=80=99ve never been a sena=
tor, but it=E2=80=99s better to be in the minority in the Senate than in th=
e House, where you literally have no power. Because of the filibuster, ther=
e=E2=80=99s still a role for senators on things like legislation and approp=
riations, but there=E2=80=99s also the convening and messaging power of a s=
enator. A senator can often corral all of those disparate groups into somet=
hing more comprehensive and more strategic. And being one of 100 in a syste=
m where you don=E2=80=99t have Democrats in the White House or in charge in=
 the House means that you have a voice in the conversation just by showing =
up to work every day.

The filibuster was very out of fashion in the Democratic
Party two years ago. Is there a feeling of relief among your future colleag=
ues that it=E2=80=99s still in place?

To be honest, what I=E2=80=99m concerned about is that President Trump is g=
oing to come into the White House and be sworn in and start wanting all his=
 initiatives to be passed, and he=E2=80=99s going to put real pressure on S=
enator Thune and others to get rid of the filibuster. I think we=E2=80=99re=
 still in a to-be-determined position on the filibuster, because I can=E2=
=80=99t imagine that President Trump will be satisfied if he can=E2=80=99t =
get things passed with the narrow majority it looks like they=E2=80=99ll ha=
ve in the House. I think there are mixed feelings about what could happen t=
o the filibuster.

The 2026 Map

You ran a campaign in Michigan and you won. The national ticket did not. Wh=
at is the one thing you wish your fellow Democrats understood about this el=
ection?

If I had to boil it down to one thing, it=E2=80=99s that our priorities nee=
d to reflect the priorities of the majority of citizens. That tends to be e=
ither their pocketbook or their kids. And we need to talk about those issue=
s, not from the faculty lounge, but from the manufacturing floor.

Do you think Democrats are ready to listen to that message?

There=E2=80=99s plenty of people saying it, but I come from the Pentagon, a=
nd when you have an operation that goes wrong, you do an after-action revie=
w, and then a lessons-learned process, and you change protocols for the nex=
t operation. Right now, there=E2=80=99s certainly a number of people who ac=
cept that we need to be
doing the after-action review. The question is, what do you do with that in=
formation, [and] once you=E2=80=99ve come to consensus, how do you operatio=
nalize that? What are your lessons learned that you=E2=80=99re going to cha=
nge in the next cycle or the next year? It=E2=80=99s that second part to me=
 that=E2=80=99s important, because otherwise we=E2=80=99re just admiring th=
e problem.

You and a handful of other Democratic senators and senators-elect managed t=
o punch through and win, and Democrats did not do badly on the House side, =
either. One of my theories is maybe it all mitigated a wave. How did you se=
e it from your vantage point?

Certainly, there was a wave going on across the country, and surviving it t=
ook real work. And it took reading the situation accurately, which means un=
derstanding that people=E2=80=99s number one issue was the economy and infl=
ation and the future of work. And if you weren=E2=80=99t talking about thos=
e things, you were having half a conversation. And it=E2=80=99s not
that other issues aren=E2=80=99t important. Of course I=E2=80=99m concerned=
 about democracy. Of course I=E2=80=99m concerned about equality and rights=
 and all those issues. But people had a hard time focusing on those issues =
if they couldn=E2=80=99t make ends meet.

And I think those of us who survived internalized that and talked about tha=
t. I think about the number of times I had to push back on some Harvard eco=
nomist who was saying that the economy was great, and actually everyone was=
 wrong, and all these millions of Michiganders just didn=E2=80=99t understa=
nd their own pocketbook. I mean, I called B.S. on that over and over and ov=
er again in the past 19 months.

How are you feeling about the midterms? Are Democrats run down and exhauste=
d, or are they starting to perk up about opportunities in the future?

We=E2=80=99re literally two weeks from the election. It=E2=80=99s a little =
early. We haven=E2=80=99t finished those postmortems; we haven=E2=80=99t fi=
gured out how we=E2=80=99re
going to change. So I think it=E2=80=99s just a little bit early to feel th=
at enthusiasm. What is important, though, is people are not sitting around =
depressed. They=E2=80=99re thinking about how to put some gas in the tank n=
ow, so that they are ready to push back when needed when Trump is sworn in.

           <p style=3D"margin: 0; color: #000000; font-size: 16px; line-hei=
ght: 30px; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 0;">Welcome back to The Best an=
d the Brightest, I=E2=80=99m <b>Abby Livingston</b>. <br/><br/>In tonight=
=E2=80=99s edition, my interview with Rep. <b>Elissa Slotkin</b>, the Democ=
ratic senator-elect who won where the top of her ticket lost: Michigan. As =
a former C.I.A. analyst and Pentagon official, Slotkin will likely be a <i>=
key</i> Democratic voice in the coming confirmation battles of both <b>Tuls=
i Gabbard</b> and <b>Pete Hegseth</b>. And she had some <i>very </i>frank o=
pinions to share about Gabbard. <br/><br/><i>But first=E2=80=A6.</i><br/><b=
r/></p><ul style=3D"margin:0;color:#000000;font-size:16px;line-height:30px;=
margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;list-style-type:square;mso-special-format:bull=
et"><li><i>Dylan on Joe & Mika=E2=80=99s Mar-a-Lago adventure</i>: On M=
onday morning, MSNBC=E2=80=99s connubial co-hosts <b>Joe Scarborough </b>an=
d <b>Mika Brzezinski</b> informed their loyal audience of
almost a million viewers that they had recently ventured south from their h=
ome in Jupiter to Mar-a-Lago. There, for the first time in seven years, the=
y met with <b>Donald Trump</b>=E2=80=94the very man they=E2=80=99d spent th=
e last <i>several years </i>vociferously pillorying. Reading from their res=
pective teleprompters with well-choreographed solemnity, the couple conveye=
d this relatively benign news as though they were disclosing their involvem=
ent in a top-secret nuclear negotiation. Or, as CNN=E2=80=99s <b>John Berma=
n </b>cheekily put it over on the rival network, =E2=80=9Cas if it were the=
 Yalta summit.=E2=80=9D<br/><br/>Anticipating some blowback from their most=
ly liberal coalition, Joe and Mika cast the Trump meeting as an attempt to =
foster a constructive dialogue. =E2=80=9CFor those asking why we would go s=
peak to the president-elect during such fraught times, especially between u=
s, I guess I would ask back, =E2=80=98Why wouldn=E2=80=99t we?=E2=80=99=E2=
=80=9D Mika said. =E2=80=9CJoe and I realized it=E2=80=99s time to do somet=
hing
different, and that starts with not only talking about Donald Trump, but al=
so talking <i>with</i> him.=E2=80=9D Later, Mika also alluded to her father=
, <b>Zbigniew</b> <b>Brzezinski</b>, a top U.S. diplomat who served as <b>J=
immy Carter</b>=E2=80=99s national security advisor. She explained that mai=
ntaining an open dialogue was =E2=80=9Ca task shared by reporters and comme=
ntators alike.=E2=80=9D<br/><br/>In light of Joe and Mika=E2=80=99s relativ=
ely exalted status in the legacy media firmament, the disclosure became a m=
inor source of fascination or frustration among their peers at 30 Rock, as =
well as fodder for the media=E2=80=99s broader introspective post-election =
soul-searching journey. At MSNBC, weekend host <b>Katie Phang </b>wrote tha=
t =E2=80=9Cnormalizing Trump is a bad idea. Period,=E2=80=9D while <b>Steph=
anie Ruhle </b>sought to convince her viewers that engagement was sound jou=
rnalistic practice, and by no means a capitulation. Joe and Mika=E2=80=99s =
pilgrimage earned write-ups from the <i>Times</i>, the
<i>Journal</i>, CNN, and so forth. It also induced predictable hysterics fr=
om the chronically outraged gasbag class, including <b>Keith Olbermann </b>=
and <b>Megyn Kelly</b>. Meanwhile, the <i>Morning Joe </i>audience, already=
 at a post-election nadir, dropped 17 percent in the hour after Joe and Mik=
a revealed their Trump meeting. The following day, the show=E2=80=99s ratin=
gs were down <i>38 percent</i> from this year=E2=80=99s average.<br/><br/>A=
las, the debate over statecraft vs. surrender diverted attention from the <=
i>real</i> palace intrigue. After all, Joe and Mika=E2=80=99s paeans to dip=
lomacy belied the true motivation for their visit to Mar-a-Lago. In fact, t=
he couple made the trek to Palm Beach because they feared retribution, a fa=
ct that has thus far only been alluded to by CNN=E2=80=99s <b>Brian Stelter=
</b>. This week, sources with direct knowledge of Joe and Mika=E2=80=99s th=
inking provided me with more details on those fears that, in addition to un=
derscoring the batshit-crazy nature of this
moment in American politics, also offer insight into the potential chilling=
 effect that Trump=E2=80=99s return to the Oval Office might have on the ne=
ws media writ large, and especially MSNBC. =E2=80=94<i>Dylan Byers</i> [<u>=

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;"><b><i>Abby Livingst=
on</i>:</b> <b>You were an acting assistant secretary at the Pentagon. Can =
you weigh in on the Pete Hegseth nomination</b>? <br/><br/><i>Elissa Slotki=
n</i>: Look, as someone who will be sworn in this January, I take very seri=
ously the constitutional role to provide advice and consent on nominations,=
 and I=E2=80=99m going to take each nomination individually and take them e=
ach very seriously. But I=E2=80=99m also someone who has served in places l=
ike the Pentagon, and at the C.I.A. I was like employee number five when we=
 created the Director of National Intelligence under <b>George W. Bush</b>.=
 I=E2=80=99ve seen these jobs up close, and they require people who are of =
character and competence, and that=E2=80=99s going to be my standard for lo=
oking at all of these nominations, and that includes having a thorough back=
ground check. <br/><br/>We=E2=80=99re looking
for people who aren=E2=80=99t going to use these positions for political re=
tribution, but who are willing to speak truth to power, which is important =
in these roles. Because it=E2=80=99s not just a parlor game, right? For Was=
hington, <i>who=E2=80=99s going to get what job </i>is like our version of =
<i>Us</i> magazine or whatever. But I  know that if you put the wrong peopl=
e in these national security roles, Americans can die here and abroad. If y=
ou get someone who=E2=80=99s not of character or competence, and on top of =
that refuses to speak truth to the commander in chief, you=E2=80=99re liter=
ally putting American lives at risk. And I won=E2=80=99t support that. But =
I=E2=80=99m going to have to wait to see what actually happens once I=E2=80=
=99m sworn in, and I=E2=80=99ll take each nomination on its own. <br/><br/>=
<b>The Tulsi Gabbard nomination has sent shockwaves through the intelligenc=
e community. As an ex C.I.A. officer, what do you think? How are your conta=
cts in the intelligence community reacting to it? </b><br/><br/>We
can=E2=80=99t have people walking into the Situation Room and telling the c=
ommander in chief what he wants to hear, particularly when it comes to our =
adversaries and things like strategic weapons and invasions of other countr=
ies. You just can=E2=80=99t have that. There has to be something more impor=
tant than political loyalty, and some of the decisions that Tulsi has made =
over the last five or eight years have confused the hell out of me. I=E2=80=
=99m not saying America is perfect when we act abroad, but any day of the w=
eek, I prefer American leadership over Russian leadership or Chinese leader=
ship. Remember, for the D.N.I., you=E2=80=99re in charge of all 18 intellig=
ence-community agencies. She has expressed views that seem to preference ad=
versaries. Certainly, it gave me pause, when I heard the nomination.  <br/>=
<br/>The intelligence community, and folks at the Pentagon, are deeply hesi=
tant about the future, and what kind of political litmus tests they=E2=80=
=99re going to be expected to take in the
early days of the Trump administration to prove their loyalty=E2=80=94and t=
herefore, what critical, classified information is actually going to get to=
 the leaders of those agencies and the commander in chief. <br/><br/>I=E2=
=80=99ve definitely heard from our allies, our English-speaking cousins, wh=
at is called the =E2=80=9CFive Eyes=E2=80=9D=E2=80=94 us, the Canadians, th=
e Brits, the Aussies, the New Zealanders. We share a tremendous amount of i=
ntelligence collection and analysis with our English-speaking cousins, and =
I certainly have heard concerns about what it means for them, their assets,=
 their intelligence collection, what kind of things are going to be at risk=
 if they disagree with someone who=E2=80=99s in charge of the D.N.I. office=
 or the C.I.A. <br/><br/><b>Do you think the Five Eyes can stay intact over=
 the next four years? </b><br/><br/>Yes. It stayed intact in the first Trum=
p administration, but I think everyone=E2=80=99s really going to be holding=
 their breath for the first three to six months.
<br/></td>

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;"><b>You=E2=80=99ll b=
e in the minority as an incoming senator. What role can a senator play in p=
ushing back against Trump?</b><br/><br/>I can=E2=80=99t speak from experien=
ce, since I=E2=80=99ve never been a senator, but it=E2=80=99s better to be =
in the minority in the Senate than in the House, where you literally have n=
o power. Because of the filibuster, there=E2=80=99s still a role for senato=
rs on things like legislation and appropriations, but there=E2=80=99s also =
the convening and messaging power of a senator. A senator can often corral =
all of those disparate groups into something more comprehensive and more st=
rategic. And being one of 100 in a system where you don=E2=80=99t have Demo=
crats in the White House or in charge in the House means that you have a vo=
ice in the conversation just by showing up to work every day.<br/><br/><b>T=
he filibuster was very out of fashion in the Democratic
Party two years ago. Is there a feeling of relief among your future colleag=
ues that it=E2=80=99s still in place? </b><br/><br/>To be honest, what I=E2=
=80=99m concerned about is that President Trump is going to come into the W=
hite House and be sworn in and start wanting all his initiatives to be pass=
ed, and he=E2=80=99s going to put real pressure on <b>Senator</b> <b>Thune<=
/b> and others to get rid of the filibuster. I think we=E2=80=99re still in=
 a to-be-determined position on the filibuster, because I can=E2=80=99t ima=
gine that President Trump will be satisfied if he can=E2=80=99t get things =

uster.<br/></td>

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;"><b>You ran a campai=
gn in Michigan and you won. The national ticket did not. What is the one th=
ing you wish your fellow Democrats understood about this election? </b><br/=
><br/>If I had to boil it down to one thing, it=E2=80=99s that our prioriti=
es need to reflect the priorities of the majority of citizens. That tends t=
o be either their pocketbook or their kids. And we need to talk about those=
 issues, not from the faculty lounge, but from the manufacturing floor. <br=
/><br/><b>Do you think Democrats are ready to listen to that message?</b><b=
r/><br/>There=E2=80=99s plenty of people saying it, but I come from the Pen=
tagon, and when you have an operation that goes wrong, you do an after-acti=
on review, and then a lessons-learned process, and you change protocols for=
 the next operation. Right now, there=E2=80=99s certainly a number of peopl=
e who accept that we need to be
doing the after-action review. The question is, what do you do with that in=
formation, [and] once you=E2=80=99ve come to consensus, how do you operatio=
nalize that? What are your lessons learned that you=E2=80=99re going to cha=
nge in the next cycle or the next year? It=E2=80=99s that second part to me=
 that=E2=80=99s important, because otherwise we=E2=80=99re just admiring th=
e problem. <br/><br/><b>You and a handful of other Democratic senators and =
senators-elect managed to punch through and win, and Democrats did not do b=
adly on the House side, either. One of my theories is maybe it all mitigate=
d a wave. How did you see it from your vantage point? </b><br/><br/>Certain=
ly, there was a wave going on across the country, and surviving it took rea=
l work. And it took reading the situation accurately, which means understan=
ding that people=E2=80=99s number one issue was the economy and inflation a=
nd the future of work. And if you weren=E2=80=99t talking about those thing=
s, you were having half a conversation. And it=E2=80=99s not
that other issues aren=E2=80=99t important. <i>Of course </i>I=E2=80=99m co=
ncerned about democracy. <i>Of course </i>I=E2=80=99m concerned about equal=
ity and rights and all those issues. But people had a hard time focusing on=
 those issues if they couldn=E2=80=99t make ends meet. <br/><br/>And I thin=
k those of us who survived internalized that and talked about that. I think=
 about the number of times I had to push back on some Harvard economist who=
 was saying that the economy was great, and actually everyone was wrong, an=
d all these millions of Michiganders just didn=E2=80=99t understand their o=
wn pocketbook. I mean, I called B.S. on that over and over and over again i=
n the past 19 months. <br/><br/><b>How are you feeling about the midterms? =
Are Democrats run down and exhausted, or are they starting to perk up about=
 opportunities in the future? </b><br/><br/>We=E2=80=99re literally two wee=
ks from the election. It=E2=80=99s a little early. We haven=E2=80=99t finis=
hed those postmortems; we haven=E2=80=99t figured out how we=E2=80=99re
going to change. So I think it=E2=80=99s just a little bit early to feel th=
at enthusiasm. What is important, though, is people are not sitting around =
depressed. They=E2=80=99re thinking about how to put some gas in the tank n=
ow, so that they are ready to push back when needed when Trump is sworn in.=
 <br/></td>
